# Claims — Range 5 (Gamble 1999)
**Source:** Clive Gamble, *The Palaeolithic Societies of Europe* (Cambridge UP, 1999). Wiki slug: `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`.  
**Range file:** `range_5_13706_17905.txt` (full-book lines 13706–17905)  
**Actual coverage:** Slice opens mid-Chapter 6 tables (accelerator dates / Interpleniglacial; OCR-garbled tables early) → readable body from Interpleniglacial (OIS 3) climate through EUP chronology, recovery environments (Kostenki, Swabian Alb, Combe Grenal, caves), Chatelperronian/Uluzzian/Aurignacian, burials, raw-material & shell transfers, *chaîne opératoire*, beads/antler/music, taskscape faunas (Mauran, mammoth, ibex) → Ch.6 Summary → opening of **Chapter 7** *The extension of social life 60,000–21,000 years ago* (power, intimate/effective/extended networks, landscape of habit, Grotte Vaufrey transfer networks). Ends mid-Table 7.2 (wheel/circle raw-material network models).  
**Prefix:** `R5-`  
**Standing rule:** All claims **as of Gamble 1999** (pre-aDNA, pre-calibration consensus as he used it). Theoretical/social-network arguments = ATTRIBUTED/POSITION. Industry ≠ people without attribution.  
**Extractor notes:** No outside knowledge; grounding quotes from this range only; no wiki edits; no task-observer (extractor subagent). OCR hyphenation repaired in quotes for readability. Early slice tables heavily OCR-garbled — load-bearing dates taken from clean prose/table text only.

**Claim count:** 62

## Claims

### R5-001 — Chapter 6 chronological frame: 60–21 Kyr bp, Middle to Upper Palaeolithic transition
- **Register:** FINDING (descriptive frame)
- **Claim:** (as of Gamble 1999) Chapter 6 frames European social life ~60,000–21,000 years ago as the transition from Middle to Upper Palaeolithic, culminating toward the last glacial maximum.
- **Attribution:** Gamble 1999
- **Quote:** "The rhythms of social life 60,000-21,000 years ago"
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]], [[gamble-clive]], [[middle-paleolithic]], [[upper-paleolithic-revolution]], [[palaeolithic-rhythms]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-002 — Interpleniglacial (OIS 3) five major interstadials 58–28 Kyr bp
- **Register:** ATTRIBUTED FINDING
- **Claim:** (as of 1999 literature as Gamble presents it) The Interpleniglacial is characterized by five major interstadials widely recorded in northern European pollen: Oerel 58–54, Glinde 51–48, Moershoofd 46–44, Hengelo 39–36, Denekamp 32–28 Kyr bp (each ~2–4 Kyr), also identifiable in the Summit Greenland ice core; they contrast with the preceding Pleniglacial (OIS 4) lack of interstadials.
- **Attribution:** Gamble citing Behre 1989; Dansgaard et al. 1993
- **Quote:** "The Interpleniglacial is characterized by five interstadials which are widely recorded in pollen profiles from the north of the continent (Table 6.3). These five major interstadials, which each lasted between 2-4 Kyr, can also be identified in the Summit ice core from central Greenland"
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999 / Behre 1989 chronology)

### R5-003 — Heinrich events revised ages (Monticchio correlation)
- **Register:** ATTRIBUTED FINDING
- **Claim:** (as of Watts et al. 1996 as Gamble reports) Six Heinrich events (massive Laurentide iceberg discharges into the N. Atlantic) are age-revised to ~14.5, 20.5, 23.4, 31.8, 41.9, 64.2 Kyr (H1–H6); significance is sudden ocean-temperature change affecting European climate; at Monticchio they correspond to high herb values / more open conditions.
- **Attribution:** Watts, Allen and Huntley 1996 via Gamble 1999
- **Quote:** "Watts et al. (1996:149-51) were able to revise the ages of the so-called six Heinrich events... They occurred at 14.5 Kyr (H1), 20.5 Kyr (H2), 23.4 Kyr (H3), 31.8 Kyr (H4), 41.9 Kyr (H5) and 64.2 Kyr (H6)."
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]], [[radiocarbon-dating]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-004 — Northern Europe Oerel: open treeless shrub-tundra, not polar desert between interstadials
- **Register:** ATTRIBUTED FINDING
- **Claim:** (as of Behre/Zagwijn as Gamble synthesizes) Netherlands/northern Europe during Oerel: open treeless shrub-tundra (Empetrum, Calluna, dwarf birch, willow, juniper); trees absent in northern Europe in Oerel and Glinde. Absence of pollen either side of interstadials does **not** mean polar desert “arctic Sahara,” but poorer plant communities; Interpleniglacial increase of grasses/herbs/Artemisia produced steppe-tundras supporting mixed large grazers.
- **Attribution:** Behre 1989; Zagwijn 1992; Guthrie 1990 via Gamble
- **Quote:** "the landscape during the Oerel interstadial, from the type site near Hamburg, is described by Behre (1989:33) as an open treeless shrub-tundra with a rich variety of herbs. ... The absence of pollen either side of these interstadials is noticeable. This does not mean that such time-periods in the north of the continent were polar deserts"
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-005 — LGM downturn phase 28–21 Kyr bp: soils, loess, cold indicators
- **Register:** FINDING (as reported synthesis)
- **Claim:** (as of Gamble 1999) Between ~28–21 Kyr bp Summit core shows weaker interstadials; continental evidence includes Briansk soil ~30–25 Kyr bp (Russian plain), Königsaue peat ~25.6 Kyr bp, increased loess/aeolian activity after ~30–20 Kyr, frost features after 25 Kyr (Kolstrup), ice advance accelerating after 25 Kyr bp.
- **Attribution:** Gamble synthesizing Hoffecker, Kozlowski, Kolstrup, Mania & Toepfer, etc.
- **Quote:** "The period between 30-20 Kyr bp is generally marked by increased aeolian activity on the northern plains as measured by the deposition of loess. ... This complicated mosaic was controlled by ice advance, which accelerated after 25 Kyr bp"
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-006 — Outline chronology of major cultural entities 60–21 Kyr bp (Table 6.5)
- **Register:** ATTRIBUTED SYNTHESIS (as of 1999 literature)
- **Claim:** (as of Gamble 1999 compiling Allsworth-Jones, Anikovich, Kozlowski, Oliva, Rigaud, Svezhentsev, Svoboda, Vega Toscano et al.) Gamble’s outline chronology includes: Late MP Mousterian of Acheulean Tradition SW 60–35, Mousterian MW 60–28; earliest UP 45–33 (Bohunician NC 44–38, Szeletian NC/SE 43–35, Proto-Aurignacian/Bachokirian SE ~43, earliest Aurignacian MW 43–33, Uluzzian MC >33, Chatelperronian SW/NW 37–33, leaf-point groups Altmühlian/Lincombian–Ranis–Jerzmanovician); EUP 33–21 (later Aurignacian all regions 33–25, Gravettian technocomplex 30–21, Kostenki–Streletskayan/Sungirian NE 33–24, Pavlovian, Willendorf/Kostenki). Division at 33 Kyr follows Desbrosse and Kozlowski 1988.
- **Attribution:** Gamble 1999 Table 6.5
- **Quote:** "An outline chronology for the archaeological cultural groups 60-21 Kyr bp ... The division at 33 Kyr bp follows Desbrosse and Kozlowski (1988)."
- **Target pages:** [[aurignacian-industry]], [[chatelperronian-industry]], [[mousterian-industry]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]], [[kostenki]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-007 — Radiocarbon moving-sum occupation peaks at ~38, 32, 27, 21.5 Kyr bp
- **Register:** ATTRIBUTED FINDING / METHOD
- **Claim:** (as of Davies & Pettitt compilation as Gamble plots) Uncalibrated C14 dates 44–21 Kyr bp plotted as 1000-year moving sum (500-year span) show four main peaks ~38, 32, 27, 21.5 Kyr bp, interpreted as recurrent fluctuations in site/population density; peak at 21.5 Kyr marks a refuge phase prior to LGM 20–18 Kyr bp. Van Andel & Tzedakis (1998) calibrate and identify maxima ~40–44 and 31–36 Cal BP with Hengelo and Denekamp.
- **Attribution:** Gamble 1999 using Davies & Pettitt data; Holdaway & Porch method; van Andel & Tzedakis 1998
- **Quote:** "When the dates are plotted in this manner as settlement data we see four main peaks at c. 38, 32, 27 and 21.5 Kyr bp. ... The peak at 21.5 Kyr bp marks a refuge phase prior to the last glacial maximum (20-18 Kyr bp)"
- **Target pages:** [[radiocarbon-dating]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-008 — N=446 C14 dates and N=202 dated locales distributed across provinces
- **Register:** FINDING (as reported)
- **Claim:** (as of Gamble’s sample) 446 radiocarbon dates and 202 dated locales distributed across North, South, Mediterranean provinces of Palaeolithic Europe; only 30–23 Kyr bp does North-Central dominate date counts; southern regions dominate locales except earliest bin (possible research bias toward dating earliest UP over Mousterian).
- **Attribution:** Gamble 1999 Tables 6.6–6.7
- **Quote:** "The sample of radiocarbon dates from which the moving sum curve was calculated has an equal distribution (Table 6.6) between the three main provinces of Europe ... Only in the period 30-23 Kyr bp does one region, the NC, dominate. ... When the number of locales is examined from which these dates were obtained, the southern regions dominate in all periods except the earliest."
- **Target pages:** [[radiocarbon-dating]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-009 — Chronological hinge after 33 Kyr bp: space-time constellations and locales as social occasions change
- **Register:** POSITION / THEORY
- **Claim:** (as of Gamble 1999) No convenient match of cultural groups to Interpleniglacial/LGM climatic episodes, but a significant chronological hinge after 33 Kyr bp: not only regional space-time material-culture constellations change but also structure of locales as social occasions.
- **Attribution:** Gamble 1999
- **Quote:** "It is possible to see that the archaeological record changes after 33 Kyr. This involves not only the character of space-time constellations at a regional scale but also corresponding developments in the structure of locales as social occasions"
- **Target pages:** [[locale]], [[palaeolithic-rhythms]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]], [[gamble-clive]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-010 — 60–33 Kyr transition is long/polythetic, not a simple “human revolution”
- **Register:** POSITION
- **Claim:** (as of Gamble 1999, citing Clarke 1968; Stringer & Gamble 1993) Period 60–33 Kyr is a long, complicated transition far from expectations raised by “the human revolution”; whether characterized as replacement or continuity, technological/typological change across Europe’s nine regions was a **polythetic** process.
- **Attribution:** Gamble 1999; Clarke 1968; Stringer and Gamble 1993
- **Quote:** "The period between 60 and 33 Kyr presents a complicated and longwinded transition, far removed from the expectations raised by phrases such as 'the human revolution'. But however the transition is characterized, either by replacement or by continuity, the data for technological and typological changes within the nine regions of Europe show it was a polythetic process"
- **Target pages:** [[upper-paleolithic-revolution]], [[behavioral-modernity]], [[modern-human-origins-debate]], [[stringer-chris]], [[gamble-clive]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-011 — Kozlowski multi-aspectual model: rupture after ~30 Kyr; only four aspects cross that line
- **Register:** ATTRIBUTED THEORY / FINDING
- **Claim:** (as of Kozlowski 1990 as Gamble presents Table 6.8) Multi-aspectual model shows considerable continuity and new traits 60–30 Kyr; after ~30 Kyr bp a definite rupture—only four aspects cross that timeline: UP blade technology, Aurignacian toolkit, unspecialized use of local raw materials, figurative art. Nine aspects cross ~40 Kyr and at least ten the ~35 Kyr boundary (transfer of social skills in technology/subsistence).
- **Attribution:** Kozlowski 1990 via Gamble 1999
- **Quote:** "after 30 Kyr bp there is a definite rupture in the record. Only four of his aspects cross this particular timeline: Upper Palaeolithic blade technology; Aurignacian tool kit; unspecialized use of local raw materials; figurative art. By contrast nine aspects cross Kozlowski's 40-Kyr bp timeline and at least ten the 35-Kyr bp boundary."
- **Target pages:** [[aurignacian-industry]], [[upper-paleolithic-revolution]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999 / Kozlowski 1990)

### R5-012 — Gamble’s two structural changes after 33 Kyr: settlement hierarchies + regional material-culture variation
- **Register:** POSITION / THEORY
- **Claim:** (as of Gamble 1999) For Gamble the two structural changes significant after 33 Kyr are **settlement hierarchies** and **pronounced inter- and intra-regional variation in material culture**, interpretable as new social skills; conceptual distinction (developed Ch.7) between landscape of habit and social landscape.
- **Attribution:** Gamble 1999
- **Quote:** "the two structural changes which strike me as significant after 33 Kyr are settlement hierarchies and the pronounced inter- and intra-regional variation in material culture. These aspects can be interpreted in terms of new social skills. The conceptual distinction ... is between the landscape of habit and the social landscape."
- **Target pages:** [[locale]], [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]], [[release-from-proximity]], [[gamble-clive]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-013 — Regionality of late Mousterian and leaf-point assemblages is real, not dating artifact
- **Register:** POSITION
- **Claim:** (as of Gamble 1999) Palaeolithic Europe’s regionality is very apparent 60–33 Kyr: late Mousterian highly distinctive within and between regions; small leaf-point assemblages mixing Middle and Upper Palaeolithic techniques are another expression; this regionality is not merely an artifact of better dating/more data.
- **Attribution:** Gamble 1999
- **Quote:** "The regionality of Palaeolithic Europe is very apparent in this period. The late Mousterian is often highly distinctive both within regions and between them. The small leaf point assemblages with their mix of Middle and Upper Palaeolithic techniques are another clear expression of such patterning. Nor does this regionality in the distribution of material culture appear to be an artifact of just better dating and more data."
- **Target pages:** [[mousterian-industry]], [[middle-paleolithic]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-014 — Recovery environments change: lakes and shorelines no longer flagship locales
- **Register:** FINDING (as reported)
- **Claim:** (as of Gamble 1999) Pre-33 Kyr complicated picture may reflect transition *or* change in normal recovery environments: lakes and shorelines that preserved flagship sites earlier no longer do so—few major lacustrine locales like Hoxne/Neumark-Nord; no shoreline landscapes like Boxgrove/Terra Amata. Shorelines partly submerged by lower sea level; Taborin identifies Biscay-shelf shells in inland SW French UP locales.
- **Attribution:** Gamble 1999; Taborin 1993
- **Quote:** "there are two environments which, having preserved a number of flagship sites and many dredgers in earlier periods, do so no longer. These are lakes and shorelines. ... The rarity of shoreline locales is easier to explain since a lowering of sea level at this time means that most of them are currently submerged. But we have evidence that they were used. Taborin (1993) has identified many shells from the exposed Biscay shelf"
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]], [[locale]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-015 — Kostenki–Borshevo complex: 25 major locales, colluvial/loess preservation, mammoth-bone constructions
- **Register:** FINDING (as reported)
- **Claim:** (as of Gamble 1999 summarizing Praslov, Rogachev, Anikovich, Klein) Around Kostenki and Borshevo on the Don (NE region): ~25 major locales over 6 km in three clusters; colluvial/loess loams over humic beds with volcanic ash; pits, mammoth-bone constructions, post holes, possibly subterranean dwellings; ideal bone preservation. No other Palaeolithic locales even flint scatters known nearby; nearest major locale Avdeevo ~300 km west.
- **Attribution:** Gamble 1999 (Kostenki literature)
- **Quote:** "Around the villages of Kostenki and Borshevo on the west bank of the Don river in Russia ... Altogether there are twenty-five major locales spread over 6km in three main clusters. ... In both locations, ravine and lower terrace, evidence exists for pits, constructions using mammoth bones, numerous post holes and possibly subterranean dwellings. ... Despite searching, no other Palaeolithic locales, not even a flint scatter, are known in the immediate Kostenki area ... The nearest locale is Avdeevo, 300km to the west"
- **Target pages:** [[kostenki]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]], [[locale]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-016 — Kostenki 1/1 excavations revolutionized Palaeolithic settlement scale (Efimenko, Rogachev)
- **Register:** ATTRIBUTED HISTORIOGRAPHIC FINDING
- **Claim:** (as of Gamble 1999) Pioneering 1930s excavations at Kostenki (Efimenko; Kostenki 1 horizon 1) are classics; Rogachev plan shows main excavation ~760 m², subsequent work doubled; with Predmosti, important for area excavation and horizontal (not only stratigraphic) information.
- **Attribution:** Gamble citing Efimenko 1958; Rogachev 1957; Praslov & Rogachev 1982
- **Quote:** "at Kostenki, where the pioneering excavations during the 1930s by Efimenko (1958), and especially at Kostenki 1 horizon 1, are justly regarded as classics for their time. One of their main effects was to revolutionize the scale at which Palaeolithic settlements were conceived. The plan published by Rogachev (1957:figure 3) shows the main area of excavation to be 760m²."
- **Target pages:** [[kostenki]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-017 — Predmosti Gravettian ~26.3–26.8 Kyr bp; mass grave ≥20 individuals destroyed WWII
- **Register:** ATTRIBUTED FINDING
- **Claim:** (as of Absolon & Klima / Svoboda as Gamble reports) Predmosti (Moravia): main archaeological horizon EUP Gravettian C14 26.3–26.8 Kyr bp; settlement from MP (OIS 5) onward; Upper Palaeolithic mass grave found 1894 by Maška with ≥20 individuals destroyed by fire end of WWII; depositional conditions differ MP vs UP but do not alone explain different archaeological character.
- **Attribution:** Absolon and Klima 1977; Svoboda et al. 1994 via Gamble
- **Quote:** "The main archaeological horizon is an Early Upper Palaeolithic Gravettian occupation which has been radiocarbon dated to 26.3-26.8 Kyr bp ... including an Upper Palaeolithic mass grave found in 1894 by Maska, and which contained at least twenty individuals ... were destroyed by fire at the end of World War II."
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]], [[radiocarbon-dating]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-018 — First Alpine occupation this period; Cotencher Mousterian 43.2 Kyr bp
- **Register:** ATTRIBUTED FINDING
- **Claim:** (as of Gamble 1999) This period sees first occupation of Alpine region: Cotencher (660 m, Switzerland) ~400 Mousterian artifacts dated 43.2 Kyr bp; Potocka Zijalka (Slovenia, 1,700 m) has EUP artifacts.
- **Attribution:** Le Tensorer 1993; Allsworth-Jones 1990 via Gamble
- **Quote:** "This period sees the first occupation of the Alpine region ... The cave of Cotencher at an altitude of 660m near Lake Neuchatel in Switzerland has a collection of 400 Mousterian artifacts dated to 43.2 Kyr bp ... Potocka Zijalka in Slovenia lies at an altitude of 1,700m and contains Early Upper Palaeolithic artifacts"
- **Target pages:** [[mousterian-industry]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-019 — Famous “transitional” central/eastern caves: tiny collections, specialized/intermittent settlements
- **Register:** ATTRIBUTED POSITION
- **Claim:** (as of Allsworth-Jones 1990 as Gamble endorses) Despite celebrity in MP–UP transition literature (Kents Cavern, Mauern, Ranis, Jerzmanovice, Szeleta, Istállós-kő, Jankovich, etc.), these caves produced numerically tiny collections from extensive excavations; Allsworth-Jones: specialized, intermittent, mostly temporary settlements. Gamble: unfortunate basis for charting the transition; better to focus larger >40 Kyr Moravian open locales (Bohunician, Szeletian, Aurignacian).
- **Attribution:** Allsworth-Jones 1990; Gamble 1999; Desbrosse & Kozlowski; Svoboda & Simán
- **Quote:** "all nine transitional sites in Table 6.10 clearly give the impression of having constituted specialised, intermittent, and for the most part strictly temporary settlements. (Allsworth-Jones 1990:196) ... It is therefore unfortunate that they have been used to form the basis for charting the transition ... It would be more sensible ... to concentrate on the larger collections of material dated to >40 Kyr bp in the open locales of Moravia."
- **Target pages:** [[aurignacian-industry]], [[middle-paleolithic]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-020 — Leaf points key to central/eastern transition talk; bone points trademark Aurignacian
- **Register:** ATTRIBUTED FINDING
- **Claim:** (as of Gamble 1999) Leaf points are a key criterion for talking about transition in central/eastern Europe—with MP at Mauern (Altmühlian type site) and UP tech/typology at Szeleta (Szeletian) and Kents Cavern (Lincombian). Bone/antler projectile points are trademark of Aurignacian (undisputed UP); outnumber retouched stone tools at Istállós-kő level 9; absent from leaf-point assemblages and Mousterian; also absent Bacho Kiro 11 Bachokirian despite Aurignacian features.
- **Attribution:** Gamble 1999; Müller-Beck; Campbell; Kozlowski 1982
- **Quote:** "the presence of leaf points is often a key element in talking about the transition in central and eastern Europe ... Bone and antler points are a trademark of the Aurignacian, which is an undisputed Upper Palaeolithic industry. ... They are also absent in Bacho Kiro level 11 where a rich Upper Palaeolithic assemblage with Aurignacian features has been called the Bachokirian"
- **Target pages:** [[aurignacian-industry]], [[mousterian-industry]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-021 — Cryoturbation can create false “levels” and primitive-looking leaf points (Hahn; Szeleta)
- **Register:** ATTRIBUTED METHODOLOGICAL CLAIM
- **Claim:** (as of Hahn 1987; Allsworth-Jones as Gamble reports) Geißenklösterle experience: cryoturbation moving artifacts can produce more levels than truly existed—refitting is key. At Szeleta cryoturbation misled typologists into regarding artifacts as “primitive.” Transition at best polythetic, at worst untraceable with such data.
- **Attribution:** Hahn 1987; Allsworth-Jones 1986; Gamble 1999
- **Quote:** "It is Hahn's opinion (1987), based on his experience of excavating the Geißenklösterle cave in southern Germany, that cryoturbation by moving artifacts through the sediments has produced more levels than truly existed. Refitting is the key to understanding the problem. ... At Szeleta, the effect of cryoturbation on artifacts misled some typologists into regarding them as primitive ... the transition was at best polythetic in character and at worst cannot be traced with such data."
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]], [[chaine-operatoire]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-022 — Swabian Alb: Aurignacian earliest UP after Hengelo; few open locales; site densities low
- **Register:** ATTRIBUTED FINDING
- **Claim:** (as of Gamble 1999 / his earlier inventory) In southern Germany Aurignacian is earliest UP and only Palaeolithic presence after Hengelo; Vogelherd and Geißenklösterle significant cave collections; no significant open locales within <200 km (nearest Lommersum, Breitenbach, Krems-Hundssteig >300 km). Gamble 1986 inventory: 42 MP and 21 EUP sites in ~15,600 km² → densities 0.0028 and 0.0014 locales/km².
- **Attribution:** Gamble 1986a / 1999; Hahn; Riek
- **Quote:** "In southern Germany the Aurignacian is the earliest Upper Palaeolithic and represents the only Palaeolithic presence in the area after the Hengelo interstadial. ... The nearest open locales are Lommersum ... All three are more than 300 km distant from the Vogelherd. ... I counted 42 Middle Palaeolithic sites and 21 Early Upper Palaeolithic (Aurignacian and Gravettian) sites from an area of some 15,600km², giving densities of 0.0028 and 0.0014 locales per km² respectively."
- **Target pages:** [[aurignacian-industry]], [[locale]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]], [[gamble-clive]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-023 — Swabian Alb cave placement: Gamble plateau productivity vs Müller-Beck Danube valley base-camp model
- **Register:** POSITION (Gamble vs Müller-Beck)
- **Claim:** (as of Gamble 1999) Continuity of cave occupation mid-plateau near Danube (not scarp edge)—Gamble explains by productive flat landscapes/grazing (site-catchment logic). Müller-Beck places Danube valley as most productive and draws hypothetical valley base camps (none found), redefining caves as satellite locales. Gamble regards Danube as marshy/difficult for grazers. Dispute illustrates interpretive change vs earlier chapters’ periods.
- **Attribution:** Gamble 1978–86a vs Müller-Beck 1988
- **Quote:** "Those caves with both Middle and Early Upper Palaeolithic occupation are consistently located in the middle of the flat limestone plateaux close to the Danube ... Müller-Beck (1988), however, draws a different conclusion ... He even draws two hypothetical base camps in the valley, even though no sites of this period have yet been found there. ... By the same token I would regard the Danube Valley as difficult, marshy country which most grazing species would avoid"
- **Target pages:** [[locale]], [[gamble-clive]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-024 — Combe Grenal Mousterian succession: Ferrassie → Quina → MTA; climate associations (Mellars, Rolland, Turq)
- **Register:** ATTRIBUTED FINDING (Binford–Bordes related chronology)
- **Claim:** (as of Mellars 1969–96; Rolland 1990; Turq 1992b as Gamble presents) In SW sequences, Quina assemblages (generally OIS 4, >60 Kyr bp per Turq) always stratified above Ferrassie; MTA type A (triangular bifaces) and B (naturally backed knives, no bifaces) always latest when present (e.g. Combe Grenal). Rolland: Denticulate commonly with milder climates (~70%, n=46); Quina/Ferrassie Charentian predominantly severe climates (>80%, n=70). Chronological patterning makes climate–resource–assemblage association more plausible.
- **Attribution:** Mellars; Rolland; Turq via Gamble 1999
- **Quote:** "He demonstrated on stratigraphic grounds that Quina assemblages ... are always stratified above the Ferrassie variant. Moreover, when present, the Mousterian of Acheulian Tradition (MTA) type A, with triangular bifaces, and type B with none but with naturally backed knives, is always the latest in the stratified sequences, as at Combe Grenal"
- **Target pages:** [[mousterian-industry]], [[acheulean-industry]], [[binford-bordes-mousterian-debate]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-025 — Périgord chronostratigraphy needs independent dating; break at Würm II/III
- **Register:** ATTRIBUTED METHODOLOGICAL CLAIM
- **Claim:** (as of Gamble 1999) Laville chronostratigraphy of Périgord cave sediments revised with C14/TL and correlated to Grande Pile/Les Echets, but no single site has complete sequence; abrupt break at Würm II/III—sequences end (Combe Grenal, Pech de l’Azé I) or begin after (Abri Pataud, Roc de Combe). Laville: all cave sediments cold-climate; interstadials = erosion. Farrand, Brunnacker, Butzer disagree; Périgord scheme needs independent dating framework.
- **Attribution:** Laville et al.; Farrand; Brunnacker; Butzer via Gamble
- **Quote:** "An abrupt break ... exists in all the cave stratigraphies. Sequences either end at the Würm II/III boundary, as at Combe Grenal and Pech de l'Azé I, or begin after it as at Abri Pataud and Roc de Combe. ... The chronostratigraphy of the Périgord therefore requires an independent dating framework rather than correlations drawn between cave sequences."
- **Target pages:** [[radiocarbon-dating]], [[thermoluminescence-dating]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-026 — Chatelperronian 37–33 Kyr bp; St Césaire Neanderthal + TL 36.3 Kyr bp
- **Register:** ATTRIBUTED FINDING
- **Claim:** (as of Harrold 1989; Lévêque et al.; Mercier et al. as Gamble reports) Small number of Chatelperronian assemblages ~37–33 Kyr bp; Neanderthal skeleton St Césaire layer 8 associated with Chatelperronian (curved blunted backed points/knives), TL 36.3 Kyr bp (Mercier et al. 1991: 36.3±2.7). Geography from Grotte du Renne (Arcy-sur-Cure, NW) to El Pendo and Cueva Morín (Cantabria). Continuity in basic flake production with late Mousterian plus more blade emphasis; main characteristic small assemblage size.
- **Attribution:** Harrold 1989; Lévêque, Backer & Gilbaud 1993; Mercier et al. 1991 via Gamble
- **Quote:** "the small number of Chatelperronian assemblages which are found between 37 and 33 Kyr bp ... The Neanderthal skeleton at St Césaire layer 8 ... is associated with a Chatelperronian assemblage with its characteristic curved, blunted backed points or knives ... and dated by TL to 36.3 Kyr bp. ... while there is continuity in basic flake production between late Mousterian and Chatelperronian, there is also more emphasis on making blades in the latter"
- **Target pages:** [[chatelperronian-industry]], [[neanderthals]], [[thermoluminescence-dating]], [[modern-human-origins-debate]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-027 — Assemblage diversity: no fine-scale break MP–EUP; richness break Chatelperronian–Aurignacian; major evenness change later Gravettian
- **Register:** ATTRIBUTED FINDING
- **Claim:** (as of Simek & Price 1990; Harrold Table 6.14 as Gamble reports) Statistical analysis: no fine-scale breaks in assemblage diversity between Mousterian and EUP. Richness shows break between Chatelperronian and Aurignacian (Chatelperronian mean 33.6 types, 281.8 tools; Early Aurignacian 42.1 types, 617.6 tools). Major change in evenness of assemblage composition occurs later in Upper Perigordian/Gravettian.
- **Attribution:** Simek and Price 1990; Harrold 1989 via Gamble
- **Quote:** "Their statistical analysis showed no fine-scale breaks in assemblage diversity between the Mousterian and the Early Upper Palaeolithic. Interestingly, richness in assemblages ... does show a break between the Chatelperronian and the Aurignacian ... But the major change in the evenness of assemblage composition occurs later, in the Upper Perigordian/Gravettian."
- **Target pages:** [[chatelperronian-industry]], [[aurignacian-industry]], [[mousterian-industry]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-028 — Uluzzian: southern Italy transitional industry with crescents; MP reduction + bone tools
- **Register:** ATTRIBUTED FINDING
- **Claim:** (as of Palma di Cesnola; Mussi as Gamble reports) Uluzzian distinctive Mediterranean-central transitional industry, some similarity to Chatelperronian (crescent-shaped backed points/knives); reduction sequences predominantly Middle Palaeolithic, many flakes, variable Levallois; bone tools at Castelcivita; confined to southern Italy (more restricted than Chatelperronian); Castelcivita only uncontentious Uluzzian C14 dates; Cavallo three Uluzzian stages with decrease of MP forms.
- **Attribution:** Palma di Cesnola; Mussi 1992; Cioni et al. via Gamble
- **Quote:** "the Mediterranean-central region has also produced a distinctive transitional industry, the Uluzzian ... This bears some similarity to the Chatelperronian in that it contains crescent-shaped backed points/knives ... The reduction sequences are however predominantly Middle Palaeolithic, with many flakes and a variable use of levallois technique. ... The Uluzzian is confined to southern Italy ... and has a more restricted distribution than the Chatelperronian"
- **Target pages:** [[chatelperronian-industry]], [[levallois]], [[middle-paleolithic]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-029 — Chauvet AMS paintings 30.3–32.4 Kyr bp; Cosquer ~27 Kyr; extends cave art age ~16 kyr
- **Register:** ATTRIBUTED FINDING
- **Claim:** (as of Chauvet et al. 1995 as Gamble reports) Four AMS dates on organic materials in Chauvet Cave paintings 30.3–32.4 Kyr bp; two charcoal torch pieces 26.9 Kyr bp; discovered 1995 Pont d’Arc (Ardèche); simultaneously extended age of European cave paintings by ~16,000 years and gave Mediterranean-west its first major painting locale. Cosquer (near Nice) AMS ~27 Kyr bp. Implication: either Iberian/Périgord paintings much later (perhaps by ~20 kyr) or their preservation/dating need reconsideration.
- **Attribution:** Chauvet, Brunel Deschamps and Hillaire 1995 via Gamble 1999
- **Quote:** "Four AMS dates on organic materials contained within the paintings found in Chauvet Cave range in age from 30.3-32.4 Kyr bp ... It simultaneously extended the age of European cave paintings by 16,000 years ... The implication of these two discoveries is that either the Iberian and Périgord paintings are much later in age, perhaps by as much as 20kyr, or their preservation and dating urgently need to be reconsidered."
- **Target pages:** [[behavioral-modernity]], [[upper-paleolithic-revolution]], [[radiocarbon-dating]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-030 — Neanderthal complete skeletons concentrated SW France and Israel; carnivore disturbance hypothesis
- **Register:** POSITION / ATTRIBUTED FINDING
- **Claim:** (as of Defleur 1993; Gamble 1986a/1999) Complete/near-complete Neanderthal skeletons concentrate in SW France and Israel (Spy certain in NW; Kiik-Koba probable NE Crimea); elsewhere highly fragmented. French sample Interpleniglacial age coincides with extreme rarity of carnivores in rock shelters; SE/ME fragmentary remains (Vindija, Krapina) coincide with abundant bear/hyena. Gamble: complete bodies may reflect carnivores not denning/disturbing post-mortem remains rather than deliberate burial alone; Guattari skull = hyena den (Stiner 1994); Hortus ≥20 individuals resistant parts with leopard/bear.
- **Attribution:** Defleur 1993; Stiner 1994; Gamble 1999
- **Quote:** "the sample is small but does emphasize the concentration of finds of complete skeletons in two restricted areas, southwest France and Israel. ... The possibility exists that the complete Neanderthal bodies recovered from France and Israel may be the result of differences in carnivore behaviour. If the carnivores did not use the rock shelters for denning or cub rearing ... then they would not have disturbed the hominid bodies"
- **Target pages:** [[neanderthals]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]], [[modern-human-origins-debate]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-031 — Open-air Upper Palaeolithic burials (Sunghir, Kostenki, Dolní Věstonice–Pavlov) as cultural development
- **Register:** POSITION
- **Claim:** (as of Gamble 1999) Novelty in UP period: open-air burials at Sunghir, Kostenki, Dolní Věstonice–Pavlov (NE/NC)—seems cultural development rather than change in preservation/recovery (full discussion deferred to Ch.7).
- **Attribution:** Gamble 1999
- **Quote:** "the novelty in this period are open air burials as at Sunghir, Kostenki and Dolni Véstonice-Pavlov in the northeast and north-central regions. This seems to be a cultural development rather than a change in preservation and recovery"
- **Target pages:** [[kostenki]], [[dolni-vestonice]], [[behavioral-modernity]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-032 — Mellars on MP cultural patterning; Gamble stresses individual negotiation skills and rhythms
- **Register:** ATTRIBUTED + POSITION
- **Claim:** (as of Mellars 1996; Gamble response) Mellars: only plausible explanation for MP technological patterns is separate technological traditions fostered by variable social distance between populations—“real element of ‘cultural’ patterning.” Gamble expects this and more: unit of explanation is **individual** not group/population; skills of negotiation/interaction with objects and landscapes; rhythms of social technology address why cultures change.
- **Attribution:** Mellars 1996; Gamble 1999
- **Quote:** "the only plausible explanation for these patterns lies in the notion of separate technological traditions — i.e. separate patterns of technological development, fostered by the variable degrees of social distance maintained between the human populations involved. ... With an emphasis on the social aspects of technology I would expect this and more. In particular I would stress the individual rather than the group or population as the unit where the explanation for such variation resides."
- **Target pages:** [[binford-bordes-mousterian-debate]], [[agency]], [[palaeolithic-rhythms]], [[gamble-clive]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-033 — Late MP transfers still mostly <100 km; six 100–300 km cases in central Europe Interpleniglacial
- **Register:** ATTRIBUTED FINDING
- **Claim:** (as of Féblot-Augustins 1993 as Gamble reports) Late Middle Palaeolithic transfers in central Europe Interpleniglacial still predominantly local (<100 km), but six examples of 100–300 km transfers vs only one in earlier MP. Bohunician almost exclusively local chert from Stránská skála (max radius ~40 km)—similar to MP Blade Group and Late MP flake patterns.
- **Attribution:** Féblot-Augustins 1993; Svoboda & Simán 1989 via Gamble
- **Quote:** "Late Middle Palaeolithic transfers in central Europe during the Interpleniglacial were still predominantly local, i.e. less than 100km, but that there were six examples, as opposed to only one in the earlier Middle Palaeolithic, of transfers between 100 and 300km. ... the Bohunician ... is also almost exclusively made on local raw materials. Indeed the distribution of Bohunician industries is limited to the distribution of chert from Stranska skala which has a maximum radius of 40 km"
- **Target pages:** [[middle-paleolithic]], [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-034 — Table 6.18: average max lithic transfer distances increase through time and SW→NC
- **Register:** ATTRIBUTED FINDING
- **Claim:** (as of Féblot-Augustins 1997 data as Gamble Table 6.18) Within each region average maximum transfer distance nearly doubles from early MP to LUP; geographical trend: distances increase from oceanic SW to continental NC. Late MP SW 40.0 km (n=61), NW 56.2 (n=16), NC 119.1 (n=23); EUP SW 51.6 (n=66), NW 82.2 (n=20), NC 157.3 (n=112); LUP NC 202.6 (n=50). Still follows Geneste principle: furthest distances = very small amounts/tools—but scale of mobility increase not expected on purely ecological grounds.
- **Attribution:** Féblot-Augustins 1997; Geneste principle via Gamble
- **Quote:** "within each region the maximum distance increases through time. In each case there is almost a doubling between the early Middle Palaeolithic and the Late Upper Palaeolithic. The second trend is geographical. ... distances increase between the oceanic (SW) and continental (NC) regions of the continent. ... we are nonetheless seeing in Table 6.18 a very substantial increase in the scale of mobility that is not expected on purely ecological grounds."
- **Target pages:** [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]], [[release-from-proximity]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-035 — Distances >300 km unknown until EUP; shells begin in EUP
- **Register:** ATTRIBUTED FINDING
- **Claim:** (as of Féblot-Augustins, Floss, Roebroeks et al. as Gamble reports) Lithic transfers >300 km unknown until Early Upper Palaeolithic and almost all associated with non-lithic materials; pattern confirmed by shell movement beginning in EUP.
- **Attribution:** Féblot-Augustins 1993; Floss 1994; Roebroeks, Kolen and Rensink 1988 via Gamble
- **Quote:** "Distances of over 300km are unknown until the Early Upper Palaeolithic ... and these are almost all associated with non-lithic materials. This pattern is confirmed with the addition of data on the movement of shells which first begins in the Early Upper Palaeolithic"
- **Target pages:** [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]], [[upper-paleolithic-revolution]], [[behavioral-modernity]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-036 — Earliest UP marked by shift to finer-grained flint at greater distances (Arbreda, Arcy)
- **Register:** ATTRIBUTED FINDING
- **Claim:** (as of Soler et al.; Farizy as Gamble reports) Earliest UP in several regions shifts to finer-grained raw materials (esp. flint) often from greater distances. l’Arbreda (Catalonia): dramatic raw-material change with UP; early Aurignacian over Mousterian emphasizes abruptness. Arcy-sur-Cure Grotte du Renne: flint rises from 10% to 70% between Mousterian XI–XII and Chatelperronian Xc–Xb, matched by UP tool types and blade elements.
- **Attribution:** Soler et al. 1990; Farizy 1990 via Gamble
- **Quote:** "with the appearance of the Upper Palaeolithic there was a dramatic change in raw materials. The sequence at l'Arbreda, where a very early Aurignacian overlies the Mousterian, emphasizes the abruptness of this change. At Arcy sur Cure ... a rise from 10 to 70 per cent in the proportions of flint at Grotte du Renne between the Mousterian, levels XI and XII, and the Chatelperronian, levels Xc and Xb."
- **Target pages:** [[aurignacian-industry]], [[chatelperronian-industry]], [[mousterian-industry]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-037 — Turq Aquitaine: Aurignacian transfers greater proportion from further afield than MP/Chatelperronian
- **Register:** ATTRIBUTED FINDING
- **Claim:** (as of Turq 1993 as Gamble reports) Sample 28 Mousterian + 4 Chatelperronian: no raw material >16 km accounts for >6% of assemblage. Earliest Aurignacian (12 locales): even in flint-rich areas material 20–30 km away still 10–20%; in flint-poor areas transfers 50–80 km up to 30%. Not merely simple cultural preference.
- **Attribution:** Turq 1993 via Gamble
- **Quote:** "No raw material transferred from more than 16km away accounted for more than 6 per cent of the raw material in that locale ... people in the earliest Aurignacian in the same small region of northeast Aquitaine were transferring materials in greater proportions from further afield ... In flint-poor areas transfers of between 50 and 80km accounted for up to 30 per cent of the lithic resources"
- **Target pages:** [[aurignacian-industry]], [[chatelperronian-industry]], [[mousterian-industry]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-038 — Bacho Kiro: Aurignacian 53% flint from >120 km vs Mousterian level 13 only 13%
- **Register:** ATTRIBUTED FINDING
- **Claim:** (as of Kozlowski 1990 / 1982 as Gamble reports) Aurignacian raw-material use highly variable by site function and age; Vedrovice almost exclusive immediate hornstone; Bacho Kiro Aurignacian levels 53% flint transferred >120 km vs Mousterian level 13 only 13% over same distance—longer continental distances match Table 6.18 pattern.
- **Attribution:** Kozlowski 1990; Kozlowski 1982; Gamble 1986a
- **Quote:** "at Bacho Kiro in the southeast region the raw materials in the Aurignacian levels consist of 53 per cent flint transferred from more than 120km ... By contrast, in the Mousterian level 13 at Bacho Kiro only 13 per cent of the raw materials was flint transferred over the same distance"
- **Target pages:** [[aurignacian-industry]], [[mousterian-industry]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-039 — Major raw-material specialization after 33 Kyr especially Gravettian/Perigordian
- **Register:** ATTRIBUTED POSITION
- **Claim:** (as of Kozlowski 1990; Svoboda 1994a as Gamble presents) Earliest UP and Late MP share variety of transfer strategies within ~20 km; major change after 33 Kyr bp especially Gravettian/Perigordian: “another period of raw material specialization ... stable system of procurement of high grade materials from distant deposits.” Moravia 40–30 Kyr: no single source dominates; 30–20 Kyr: southern Polish flints much more common, fewer sources, coincides with large river settlements (Predmosti, Dolní Věstonice–Pavlov, Milovice).
- **Attribution:** Kozlowski 1990; Svoboda 1994a via Gamble
- **Quote:** "It is only during the Gravettian technocomplex in the middle phase of the Upper Palaeolithic that another period of raw material specialization sets in, with a stable system of procurement of high grade materials from distant deposits. (Kozlowski 1990:432-3) ... between 30 and 20 Kyr bp flints from southern Poland become much more common and the number of sources used in transfers declines. This coincides with the appearance of large settlements along the Moravian rivers as at Predmosti, Dolni Véstonice-Pavlov and Milovice."
- **Target pages:** [[dolni-vestonice]], [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]], [[upper-paleolithic-revolution]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-040 — Salching Gravettian: 42–51% keratophyre from ~150 km; Font Robert point
- **Register:** ATTRIBUTED FINDING
- **Claim:** (as of Weißmüller 1987; Scheer 1993 as Gamble reports) Gravettian Salching (Bavaria S of Danube): excavation n=254 includes 51% keratophyre; with plough-zone n=949 → 42%; closest source ~150 km north in Fichtelgebirge; includes tanged Font Robert point (type-fossil of Perigordian V/Gravettian; Maisières canal Belgium dates such points ~28 Kyr bp); material brought as pre-forms then knapped.
- **Attribution:** Weißmüller 1987; Scheer 1993; Rigaud 1988b via Gamble
- **Quote:** "The small stone assemblage from the excavation (n=254) comprised 51 per cent keratophyre. When combined with material collected from the plough zone (n=949) this same stone accounted for 42 per cent of the entire collection ... The closest source of keratophyre to Salching is 150km to the north in the Fichtelgebirge ... It appears that at Salching raw material was brought into the locale as pre-forms and then knapped."
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]], [[locale]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-041 — Shell transfers: Mediterranean shells to Middle Rhine ~700 km; Black Sea shells to Kostenki ~500 km
- **Register:** ATTRIBUTED FINDING
- **Claim:** (as of Floss 1994; Hahn 1977; Taborin 1993 as Gamble reports) EUP transfers shells over much larger distances than stone. Mainz-Linsenberg and Sprendlingen (Middle Rhine Gravettian): Mediterranean shore shells, direct distance ~700 km (small numbers). Floss records 72 shell transfers for entire European UP; regularly exceed 100-km landscape-of-habit limit. Aurignacian Kostenki: shells from Black Sea ~500 km. Taborin: Périgord/Lot/Gironde major shell locales; distance Abri Pataud to Pleistocene Atlantic/Mediterranean shores ~400 km; EUP majority Atlantic coast, LUP more “all shores” + fossil Miocene.
- **Attribution:** Floss 1994; Hahn 1977; Taborin 1993 via Gamble
- **Quote:** "At the Gravettian locales of Mainz-Linsenberg and Sprendlingen in the Middle Rhine there are shells from the Mediterranean shore ... The direct distance for such transfers is 700km. ... These include transfers of 500 km for shells into Aurignacian assemblages at Kostenki from the Black Sea ... While these include later Magdalenian transfers they do show ... that the 100-km limit of the landscape of habit was regularly exceeded."
- **Target pages:** [[kostenki]], [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]], [[release-from-proximity]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-042 — Aurignacian *chaîne opératoire* at Geißenklösterle: 95% local hornstein; opportunistic unipolar blades
- **Register:** ATTRIBUTED FINDING
- **Claim:** (as of Hahn 1988–91; Owen 1988 as Gamble reports) Multi-stage UP prepared-core blade production (platform prep, crested blade, unipolar/bipolar radial detachment, rejuvenation). Geißenklösterle Aurignacian: six raw-material types; only 1% from >20 km; 95% local Ach-valley hornstein; small nodules → opportunistic reduction; mostly unipolar; cores worked to produce 30–40 mm blades.
- **Attribution:** Hahn 1988, 1991; Hahn and Owen 1985; Owen 1988 via Gamble
- **Quote:** "In the Aurignacian levels at Geißenklösterle there were six raw-material types. Only one per cent of all materials used came from more than 20 km from the cave and 95 percent was from local hornstein picked up in the Ach valley. The size of nodules was small which led to an opportunistic reduction strategy ... Most of them are unipolar and the cores were worked down as far as they could be taken and still produce blades of 30-40 mm in length."
- **Target pages:** [[chaine-operatoire]], [[aurignacian-industry]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-043 — Gravettian vs Aurignacian core/blade production: more standardized, bipolar, indirect percussion
- **Register:** ATTRIBUTED FINDING
- **Claim:** (as of Hahn 1991; Owen 1988 as Gamble summarizes) Gravettian at Geißenklösterle: radiolarite from 15 km more common; cores more standardized/elaborately prepared; decrease in blade thickness; Gravettian prepares ridge before platform (vs Aurignacian platform first); bipolar/opposed platforms common in Gravettian; micro-blades for backed pieces; more indirect percussion (bone/antler punches) vs Aurignacian direct percussion. Yet dimensions highly standardized—alternatives producing standardized blade supports continuing into Early Magdalenian.
- **Attribution:** Hahn 1991; Owen 1988; Hahn and Owen 1985 via Gamble
- **Quote:** "Hahn (1991:119) has summarized the difference between the chaines opératoires in the Aurignacian and Gravettian as follows: a general decrease in blade thickness ... Bipolar cores are common in the Gravettian ... direct percussion was used predominantly in the Aurignacian, while in the Gravettian more use is made of indirect percussion ... what we have here is a set of alternatives producing a very standardized product — blade supports."
- **Target pages:** [[chaine-operatoire]], [[aurignacian-industry]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-044 — Achtal Gravettian inter-cave refits: cores moved; contrast Maastricht-Belvédère MP
- **Register:** ATTRIBUTED FINDING
- **Claim:** (as of Scheer 1990–93 as Gamble reports) Scheer nodule-exploitation model tested on Achtal Gravettian (Sirgenstein, Geißenklösterle, Brillenhöhle, Hohler Fels): eight refits between caves, Geißenklösterle–Hohler Fels almost 3 km; all three Cziesla refit categories; burin modified multiple times then carried between caves; dates Geißenklösterle Ia 23.6±0.3 and Hohler Fels IIb 21.1±0.5 / 23.1±0.07 Kyr bp; linked by ivory pendants. Cores frequently transferred; contrast Middle Palaeolithic Maastricht-Belvédère (Ch.5)—Achtal shows contemporaneity and local movement of tools/raw material within reduction sequence.
- **Attribution:** Scheer 1990, 1993 via Gamble; contrast to Maastricht-Belvédère
- **Quote:** "Scheer was also able to make eight refits between the caves which in the case of the Geißenklösterle and Hohler Fels are almost 3km apart ... This valley study shows very clearly that cores were frequently transferred around the landscape since refitting failed to conjoin large series ... This is a very different result than, for example, that from the Middle Palaeolithic locale of Maastricht-Belvédère ... In the Achtal there is evidence not only for contemporaneity but also for the local movement of tools and raw material within a local reduction sequence"
- **Target pages:** [[maastricht-belvedere]], [[chaine-operatoire]], [[locale]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-045 — Learned production sequences (*chaîne*) are the cultural products, not final tool forms
- **Register:** POSITION / THEORY
- **Claim:** (as of Gamble 1999) Against Vaquero’s Romani cognitive conclusion and building on Achtal/Turq studies: variety in UP production sequences can yield standardized supports; analytical emphasis on **process/gestures of making** (learned, transmitted social acts) rather than thoughts about final form or final form itself—learned sequences are cultural/social products, “ways of doing.”
- **Attribution:** Gamble 1999
- **Quote:** "The emphasis, quite rightly, is therefore on the gestures of making rather than the thoughts of what the knappers were making, in this instance flake and blade supports. ... The analytical emphasis must be on process rather than final form. It is those learned sequences which are the cultural and social products, the ways of doing, rather than the things produced."
- **Target pages:** [[chaine-operatoire]], [[agency]], [[gamble-clive]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-046 — Earliest pierced ornaments: Bacho Kiro level 11 earlier than Chatelperronian Arcy
- **Register:** ATTRIBUTED FINDING
- **Claim:** (as of Kozlowski 1982; White 1993b as Gamble reports) Earliest deliberate piercing for pendants/necklaces/clothing: Bacho Kiro level 11 (Aurignacian/Bachokirian)—two pierced animal teeth and spindle-shaped bone pendant; much earlier than similar items from Chatelperronian Arcy-sur-Cure. Aurignacian ornament raw materials wide-ranging (ivory, shells, teeth, jet, hematite, etc.).
- **Attribution:** Kozlowski 1982; White 1993b; Farizy; Leroi-Gourhan via Gamble
- **Quote:** "The earliest evidence for the deliberate piercing of items either for suspension as pendants or as parts of necklaces, or to allow attachment onto clothing, comes from level 11 at Bacho Kiro ... These are of course much earlier, as White (1993b:279) has pointed out, than similar pierced items from the Chatelperronian levels of Arcy sur Cure"
- **Target pages:** [[aurignacian-industry]], [[chatelperronian-industry]], [[behavioral-modernity]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-047 — Ornament geography: fox canines northern; shells common France, rare elsewhere in Aurignacian
- **Register:** ATTRIBUTED FINDING
- **Claim:** (as of Hahn 1977; Straus; Mussi; Taborin as Gamble reports) Fox canines common Aurignacian France/Belgium/Germany/Russia (SW, NC, NE); deer canines also common; but Cantabria and Mediterranean west/central: fox canines absent, deer dominate—corresponds to arctic fox *Alopex lagopus* distribution in Mammuthus–Coelodonta fauna. Shells common in France Aurignacian, very rare elsewhere in Europe.
- **Attribution:** Hahn 1977; Straus 1992; Mussi 1992; Khalke 1994 via Gamble
- **Quote:** "fox canines ... are very common in Aurignacian locales in France, Belgium, Germany and Russia ... However in the Cantabrian region ... and throughout the Mediterranean west and central regions fox canines are absent and deer canines dominate. The second pattern concerns the distribution of shells. These are common in France ... but very rare in the Aurignacian elsewhere in Europe"
- **Target pages:** [[aurignacian-industry]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-048 — Ivory bead production: ~3 hours/bead; La Souquette ~1,200 hours; possible imported rods
- **Register:** ATTRIBUTED FINDING
- **Claim:** (as of White 1989–97 as Gamble reports) Aurignacian beads gouged not finely drilled, then ground/polished; hematite as polishing abrasive. Basket-shaped beads (SW France) vs two-holed beads (Geißenklösterle) share similar rod→string production sequences. White suggests (to verify) SW ivory imported as ~10 cm rods because mammoth rare in SW fauna; German Aurignacian has numerous tusk sections. Experiment: ~3 hours per basket bead; La Souquette basal Aurignacian ~400 beads ≈ 1,200 hours—far exceeds ~20 minutes for skilled knapper to pocketful of Aurignacian stone supports. Beads/shells not part of rare pre-30 Kyr burials; abundant with Gravettian burials (Dolní Věstonice, Sunghir, Balzi Rossi).
- **Attribution:** White 1989–97; Taborin 1993 via Gamble
- **Quote:** "it takes on average three hours to make a single Aurignacian basket-shaped bead. At La Souquette there are 400 such beads in the basal Aurignacian assemblage, adding up to 1,200 hours of work. ... It is very apparent that beads and shells were not part of the very few burials which may be earlier than 30 Kyr bp. ... The abundant use of ivory beads and shells in the Gravettian/Perigordian ... is largely due to the presence of burials."
- **Target pages:** [[chaine-operatoire]], [[aurignacian-industry]], [[dolni-vestonice]], [[behavioral-modernity]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-049 — Split-based Aurignacian antler points: 97% antler; transferable wedging/grooving skills
- **Register:** ATTRIBUTED FINDING
- **Claim:** (as of Knecht 1993a–b as Gamble reports) Of 381 early Aurignacian split-based points France/Belgium/Germany, 97% antler; tongued pieces exclusively antler (likely reindeer)—wedges to expand split for hafting, not original split-makers; lashing sufficient without resin. Wedging/grooving/snapping transferrable skills across materials (bead rods, points)—habitual gestures segment of production *chaîne*. Split-base points made similarly over large areas; Gravettian single-bevel points prefer bone, streamlined spears, regional bevel choice.
- **Attribution:** Knecht 1993a, 1993b via Gamble
- **Quote:** "In her sample of 381 from France, Belgium and Germany, Knecht found that 97 per cent were made on antler ... The tongued pieces ... are explained by Knecht as wedges to expand the split and so assist hafting. ... These transferrable skills from one material to another, and from one production sequence to another, were a suite of habitual gestures, a segment in the chaine of production. ... the Aurignacian antler points were made in similar ways over large areas"
- **Target pages:** [[chaine-operatoire]], [[aurignacian-industry]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-050 — Königsaue resin AMS ~43.8 and 48.4 Kyr bp with bifacial tool impression and fingerprint
- **Register:** ATTRIBUTED FINDING
- **Claim:** (as of Hedges et al. 1998; Mania & Toepfer as Gamble reports) Two resin pieces Königsaue A and B AMS 43,800±2100 and 48,400±3700 bp; one bears negative impression of backed bifacial tool, possible wooden haft imprint, and fingerprint presumably Neanderthal. French MTA microwear shows scraper hafting polishes (Anderson-Gerfaud, Beyries); adhesives not found on French material.
- **Attribution:** Hedges et al. 1998; Mania and Toepfer 1973; Anderson-Gerfaud 1990; Beyries 1988 via Gamble
- **Quote:** "two pieces of resin from Königsaue A and B, NC region, have been AMS dated to 43,800+2100 and 48,400+3700 bp ... One piece bears the negative impression of a backed bifacial tool, the possible imprint of a wooden haft and a fingerprint, presumably of a Neanderthal"
- **Target pages:** [[neanderthals]], [[radiocarbon-dating]], [[mousterian-industry]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999 / Hedges et al. 1998)

### R5-051 — Geißenklösterle swan-radius flutes AMS ~36.8 Kyr bp: possibly oldest European musical instruments
- **Register:** ATTRIBUTED FINDING / POSITION
- **Claim:** (as of Hahn & Münzel 1995 as Gamble reports) Two bone flutes from Geißenklösterle AH IIab Aurignacian AMS 36.8 Kyr bp—very possibly oldest musical instruments in Europe; best preserved on swan radius with three sound holes and seven transversal cuts; not special burial/hut context. Isturitz: 1 Aurignacian + 9 Gravettian + 6 Perigordian V flutes. Gamble: formal music and formal depiction use additional **symbolic** resources; stretch social life across time/space beyond knapping’s older mutual environment.
- **Attribution:** Hahn and Münzel 1995; Buisson 1990; Gamble 1999 interpretation
- **Quote:** "Two bone flutes have been pieced together from horizon IIab in the Geißenklösterle ... This Aurignacian level is AMS dated to 36.8 Kyr bp so that these are very possibly the oldest musical instruments in Europe. ... formal music-making and formal depictions of the world are derived from an additional, symbolic, use of resources so that it comes as no surprise that such social technology appears much later."
- **Target pages:** [[behavioral-modernity]], [[aurignacian-industry]], [[palaeolithic-rhythms]], [[gamble-clive]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-052 — MP–UP specialized faunas do not show clean behavioral break (Table 6.27)
- **Register:** POSITION / ATTRIBUTED FINDING
- **Claim:** (as of Gamble 1999; Stiner 1993) Mousterian assemblages scattered through specialized single-species fauna list rather than clustered; some levels taphonomic (cave-bear dens at Stadel/Geißenklösterle). Difficult from list to argue behavioral change between Middle and Upper Palaeolithic; prime-aged hunting long predates transition (Gaudzinski 1995). Stiner: archaic differences in strategy combination and territory use, not simply “not modern.”
- **Attribution:** Gamble 1999; Stiner 1993; Gaudzinski 1995
- **Quote:** "Mousterian assemblages are found throughout the list, rather than clustered, and a variety of different taxa and carnivores are represented. ... it would be difficult from this list to argue for a change in behaviour between the Middle and Upper Palaeolithic."
- **Target pages:** [[neanderthals]], [[middle-paleolithic]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-053 — Mauran denticulate Mousterian bison midden: catastrophic female/calf kill, end summer–autumn
- **Register:** ATTRIBUTED FINDING
- **Claim:** (as of Farizy, David & Jaubert 1994 as Gamble reports) Mauran (Haute-Garonne): 99% bison midden; MNI 83 from 25 m² of estimated 1,000 m² → potentially ~4,000 animals; raw materials within 15 km (mostly quartzite); denticulate Mousterian; topography for ambush. Tooth eruption: end summer into autumn; 80% adult females and calves; catastrophic age profile with high young ratio; only one carnivore bone, no gnawing; bones systematically smashed for marrow; no spatial patterning in midden.
- **Attribution:** Farizy, David and Jaubert 1994 via Gamble
- **Quote:** "Excavations at the aven locale of Mauran ... have produced a midden of bison bone. ... consists of 99 per cent bison. ... The MNI for bison is 83 from a small excavation of 25m² and an estimated area of 1,000m² which could be excavated. If bison were recovered at the same density over this entire area then almost 4,000 animals must be present. ... Eighty per cent of the individuals were adult females and calves. ... the age profile is described as catastrophic"
- **Target pages:** [[mousterian-industry]], [[neanderthals]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-054 — Abri Pataud Aurignacian reindeer vs Mauran: not very different organizational capabilities
- **Register:** POSITION
- **Claim:** (as of Spiess 1979; Enloe 1993; Patou-Mathis 1994 as Gamble synthesizes) Abri Pataud Aurignacian level 14: reindeer 99%, late autumn–winter Vézère refuge, prime-aged cull of small mixed herds (not mass driving). Le Flageolet I Perigordian V similar winter prime-aged reindeer; Enloe interprets single-hunter long-distance transport (gourmet/bulk strategies after Binford). Faunal evidence does **not** suggest very different strategies/organizational capabilities between Mauran (MP) and Abri Pataud (Aurignacian); Tournal MP horse / Aurignacian reindeer treated similarly (short-visit palimpsest).
- **Attribution:** Spiess; Enloe; Patou-Mathis; Binford via Gamble
- **Quote:** "The faunal evidence does not therefore suggest very different strategies or organizational capabilities between the hominids at Mauran and Abri Pataud. A similar conclusion has been reached by Patou-Mathis (1994) for the Grotte Tournal"
- **Target pages:** [[neanderthals]], [[aurignacian-industry]], [[mousterian-industry]], [[binford-lewis]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-055 — Mammoth middens after ~33/28 Kyr: Gravettian NC/NE; collected as well as hunted (Soffer)
- **Register:** ATTRIBUTED FINDING
- **Claim:** (as of Soffer 1993; Kozlowski as Gamble reports) Major difference after 33 Kyr and especially climatic downturn after 28 Kyr: locales with many mammoth remains restricted to some regions; Gravettian NC/NE mammoth middens (Dolní Věstonice I–II >150 MNI; Predmosti >1,000; Pavlov, Milovice, Spadzista, Kostenki 1/1 etc.); bones sometimes used for windbreaks/structures. Soffer: remains from kills **and** natural “mammoth graveyards”; generally lack cut marks but carnivore-gnawed—collected as much as hunted; Moravian mineral licks as attractors; mammoth bone not burnt/stored in pits at Moravian locales (contrast Kostenki 1/1 and Central Russian Plain post-LGM).
- **Attribution:** Soffer 1993; Kozlowski 1990 via Gamble
- **Quote:** "The major difference in this period occurs after 33 Kyr bp and more particularly with the downturn in climate to the LGM after 28 Kyr bp. The new aspect is locales with many mammoth remains. ... Soffer emphasizes that these mammoth remains resulted not only from hominid kills but also from natural accumulations, the so-called mammoth graveyards ... Generally the bones lack cut marks but are carnivore gnawed, which strongly suggests they were as much collected as obtained from hunted carcasses"
- **Target pages:** [[kostenki]], [[dolni-vestonice]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-056 — Ibex hunting skills transferred across MP–UP; novel LUP resource-poor mountain use
- **Register:** POSITION / ATTRIBUTED FINDING
- **Claim:** (as of Gamble 1995e/1999; Straus 1987) Among 588 faunas, 152 with Capra ibex; most abundant by period in EUP, by region in Mediterranean east. Resource-rich Community 1 (hominids + large + small carnivores) hosts ibex hunting transferred across MP–UP transition (antiquity after last interglacial ~118 Kyr). Novel LUP skills: Capra-dominated high-mountain locales with few other species—exploiting resource-poor communities with specific landscape knowledge. Faunal diversity/evenness decline after 28 Kyr (Simek & Snyder); lithic richness/evenness change with Perigordian partly explained by declining animal diversity (Simek & Price)—change **within** UP greater than between MP and UP.
- **Attribution:** Gamble 1995e, 1999; Straus 1987; Simek & Snyder 1988; Simek & Price 1990
- **Quote:** "Ibex hunting within a resource rich community was a set of skills transferred across the Middle-to-Upper Palaeolithic transition. ... Novel skills are however found after the last glacial maximum at Capra ibex-dominated locales, high in the mountains and with very few other species ... Faunal changes are therefore greater within the Upper Palaeolithic than between the Middle and Upper Palaeolithic."
- **Target pages:** [[middle-paleolithic]], [[upper-paleolithic-revolution]], [[gamble-clive]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-057 — Ch.6 summary: level playing field; continuity + innovations; major structural change after 33 Kyr
- **Register:** POSITION
- **Claim:** (as of Gamble 1999) Transition study requires level playing field (climate, survival, recovery) so differences = social change not ecology/preservation artifacts. Continuity 60–21 Kyr: assemblage size, regional occupation intensity, prime-aged hunting (Kozlowski multi-aspectual). Innovations: raw-material transfer distances, shell transfer, material representations (ornament, sculpture, music). Major structural changes after 33 Kyr. Pattern by culture: Late MP no more complex than Early MP; leaf-point/transitional industries numerically small; Aurignacian numerically small but transfer variety + display + cave painting/music; Gravettian sometimes very large (NC/NE), bulk distant materials, new burial dimension, Mediterranean shell contacts.
- **Attribution:** Gamble 1999 Summary
- **Quote:** "the playingfield on which the transition was enacted was more or less level during the period 60-21 Kyr bp. ... these continuities are also matched by innovative rhythms and social technology ... The most significant are the distance of raw material transfers, the transfer of shells and the fashioning of material representations be they ornament, sculpture or musical phrases. However, despite these innovations the major structural changes occur in the Palaeolithic record after 33 Kyr bp"
- **Target pages:** [[palaeolithic-rhythms]], [[upper-paleolithic-revolution]], [[behavioral-modernity]], [[gamble-clive]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-058 — Two social systems (Neanderthal and Cro-Magnon): symbolic system integration vs co-presence limits
- **Register:** POSITION / THEORY
- **Claim:** (as of Gamble 1999) Innovative rhythms → system integration via symbolic resources beyond co-presence limits; occurred selectively before 33 Kyr. Two different social systems—Neanderthal and Cro-Magnon—based on distinct uses of emotional, material, and symbolic resources; confirms level playing field and focuses what needs explaining in MP **replacement**; pure population-replacement portrayal misrepresents transition subtlety. Differences not primarily Stiner-style strategy/territory rules but living within environments; human **agency** creates taskscape irrespective of fossil species name-tag.
- **Attribution:** Gamble 1999
- **Quote:** "The existence of two different social systems, Neanderthal and Cro-Magnon, based on the distinct use of resources — emotional, material and symbolic — both confirms the level playingfield and focuses attention on what needs explaining in terms of Middle Palaeolithic replacement. This approach emphasizes that we will indeed misrepresent the subtlety of the transition if it is portrayed purely as one of population replacement. ... Human agency, irrespective of any fossil name-tag, created the immediate taskscape which surrounded the individual irrespective of what hominid species he or she happened to be."
- **Target pages:** [[neanderthals]], [[agency]], [[modern-human-origins-debate]], [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]], [[gamble-clive]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-059 — Ch.7 thesis: significant Palaeolithic transition requires transformation of power and extended networks
- **Register:** POSITION / THEORY
- **Claim:** (as of Gamble 1999 Ch.7) No significant transition between Palaeolithic societies without transformation of power—more than new hunting/warming/planning techniques; extends ambit of individual action. Three networks (intimate, effective, extended) defined by resources—emotional, material, symbolic—appropriate to their maintenance. Extended networks of significant size cannot rest on emotional resources (need time/attention/co-presence); symbolic resources inappropriate for intimate network (disembody action). Transition marks appearance of **extended networks** with social occasion and place within social landscapes; system integration = action *in absentia*; personal/institutional power extended across time/space.
- **Attribution:** Gamble 1999
- **Quote:** "I will argue in this chapter that there can be no significant transition between Palaeolithic societies which did not involve the transformation of power. ... I will argue that the transition marks the appearance of extended networks ... with the social occasion and the creation of place within social landscapes ... The result is system integration which deals with action in absentia ... Personal as well as institutional power is extended across time and space."
- **Target pages:** [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]], [[release-from-proximity]], [[agency]], [[locale]], [[gamble-clive]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-060 — “Release from proximity” now occurs (Rodseth et al. 1991)
- **Register:** POSITION / THEORY
- **Claim:** (as of Gamble 1999 citing Rodseth et al. 1991) Building on primate and hominid social heritage, the “release from proximity” now occurred in this transition—social life stretched beyond co-presence.
- **Attribution:** Gamble 1999; Rodseth et al. 1991
- **Quote:** "The 'release from proximity' (Rodseth et al. 1991), building on our primate and hominid social heritage, now occurred."
- **Target pages:** [[release-from-proximity]], [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]], [[gamble-clive]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-061 — Landscape of habit: 80–100 km furthest bulk transfers; EUP increases furthest distance and novel items
- **Register:** THEORY + ATTRIBUTED FINDING
- **Claim:** (as of Gamble 1999) Landscape of habit centered on individual decisions—wider spatial network for negotiation/reproduction of social life; applies to all hominids; contains rules/resources for social integration but not system integration beyond intimate/effective networks until late Pleistocene. Dimensions traced via raw-material transfers: 80–100 km furthest, majority <20 km—same Late MP and many EUP locales. EUP: significant increases in furthest distance and quantities at some locales + novel transfer items (shell, possibly ivory). Does **not** mean landscape of habit expanded in size—bulk still within model—but regular transfers beyond 80–100 km threshold and non-simple fall-off curves imply changed individual mobility patterns.
- **Attribution:** Gamble 1999; Féblot-Augustins 1997; Turq 1993
- **Quote:** "Distances of 80-100km invariably represent the furthest transfers, although the majority of materials are moved over much shorter distances, normally less than 20km. ... in the EUP there were significant increases both in furthest distance and, at some locales, in the quantities of raw material being transferred. This was combined with novel items for transfer — shell and possibly ivory. These changes do not imply that the landscape of habit expanded in size during the Aurignacian and Gravettian as opposed to the Mousterian."
- **Target pages:** [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]], [[locale]], [[gamble-clive]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)

### R5-062 — Neanderthal mobility as continuous renewal vs provisioning/places for future action
- **Register:** POSITION / THEORY
- **Claim:** (as of Gamble 1999 rephrasing Kuhn 1992) Mousterian transfers embedded in other routines; temporality of social action ~1–2 days (5–20 km); Geneste principle: distant material arrives as retouched tools. Neanderthals characterized as constantly renewing lithic technology—carrying stone habitual. Big difference: modern humans move social occasion to social occasion; Neanderthals at most gathering to gathering. Kuhn: Neanderthals carried tools but did not stock locales with caches; once provisioning occurs, social implications immense—temporality changes as places for future action created; interruptions to continuous acquisition–discard sequence. Gamble rephrases: they carried sequences of embodied gestures (artifacts) as extensions of hands, not discursive “hedge against unforeseen needs.” Applies across anatomical types with Mousterian (including some anatomically modern Middle East). Grotte Vaufrey star-pattern transfers; MP pattern closer to low-density wheel/star-burst network than seasonal circular round; individuals did not carry bulk >20 km nor pass materials hand-to-hand in bulk (would alter fall-off curves); links between locales ~5–10 km.
- **Attribution:** Gamble 1999; Kuhn 1992; Geneste 1988; Féblot-Augustins; Turq; Stringer and Gamble 1993
- **Quote:** "The big difference is that we move from social occasion to social occasion while Neanderthals moved at most from gathering to gathering. I referred earlier (Chapter 5) to Kuhn's conclusion that they regularly carried tools but did not stock locales with caches of material. Once such provisioning occurs the social implications are immense. The temporality of action changes as places for future action are created. ... The Middle Palaeolithic transfers show that these links were conditioned by distance and the temporality of action. Individuals did not carry amounts of raw materials further than 20km. ... each link between the nodes, or locales, in the wheel network must be some 5-10km in length."
- **Target pages:** [[neanderthals]], [[locale]], [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]], [[agency]], [[chaine-operatoire]], [[stringer-chris]], [[gamble-clive]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]], [[modern-human-origins-debate]]
- **Date-stamp:** (as of Gamble 1999)
